 {"id":"bd-1yo9","title":"Feature: Extension Tool Registration and Execution","description":"# Feature: Extension Tool Registration and Execution

## Overview

Extensions can register custom tools via pi.registerTool(). These tools must be:
1. Added to the agent's tool registry
2. Included in the tools array sent to the provider
3. Executable when the model invokes them

## Current State

The JS-side registration exists:
- `pi.registerTool(config)` registers a tool definition
- `__pi_execute_tool(name, callId, input)` exists in PI_BRIDGE_JS
- Tool definitions stored in extension's tools map

What's missing:
- Rust-side collection of registered tools
- Integration with agent's ToolRegistry
- Execution flow from model tool call → JS handler → result

## Architecture

```
Extension calls pi.registerTool()
            │
            ▼
JS runtime stores tool definition
            │
            ▼
Agent collects tools from all extensions ◄── [MISSING]
            │
            ▼
Tools added to ToolRegistry ◄── [MISSING]
            │
            ▼
Model receives tools in API call
            │
            ▼
Model invokes extension tool
            │
            ▼
Agent routes to extension handler ◄── [MISSING]
            │
            ▼
JS __pi_execute_tool() called ◄── [MISSING]
            │
            ▼
Result returned to model
```

## Implementation Requirements

### 1. Collect Registered Tools
After extension activation, query JS runtime for registered tools:
```rust
let ext_tools = runtime.get_registered_tools()?;
```

### 2. Create Tool Wrapper
Wrap each extension tool as an AgentTool:
```rust
struct ExtensionToolWrapper {
    name: String,
    label: String,
    description: String,
    parameters: serde_json::Value,
    runtime: Arc<PiJsRuntime>,
}

#[async_trait]
impl Tool for ExtensionToolWrapper {
    async fn execute(&self, call_id: &str, input: Value) -> Result<ToolOutput> {
        self.runtime.execute_extension_tool(&self.name, call_id, input).await
    }
}
```

### 3. Add to Registry
```rust
for tool_def in runtime.get_registered_tools()? {
    let wrapper = ExtensionToolWrapper::new(tool_def, runtime.clone());
    tool_registry.register(wrapper);
}
```

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Extension tools appear in provider's tools array
- [ ] Model can invoke extension tools
- [ ] Tool execution routes to JS handler
- [ ] Results return to model correctly
- [ ] Errors handled properly","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:59:00.168079003Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-04T19:59:00.174614141Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1yo9","depends_on_id":"bd-30zg","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:59:00.174573255Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

+{"id":"bd-1yr1","title":"Implement cancellation budgets for extension handlers","description":"Use asupersync cancellation budgets to bound cleanup time for extensions.

## Background
Cancellation budgets ensure:
- Cleanup completes in bounded time
- No indefinite waits during shutdown
- Predictable Ctrl+C behavior

## Current State
- Extensions use simple cancellation flags
- No guarantee cleanup completes quickly
- Long-running handlers may delay shutdown

## Implementation
1. Define cancellation budgets per operation type:
   - Extension event handler: 5s
   - Tool execution cleanup: 30s
   - UI dialog cancellation: 1s
2. Apply budgets via Cx::with_budget()
3. Force-cancel if budget exceeded

## Budget Configuration
Could be configurable in settings:
```json
{
  "extension_budgets": {
    "event_handler_ms": 5000,
    "tool_cleanup_ms": 30000
  }
}
```

## Test Plan
- Unit test: slow handler cancelled at budget
- Integration test: Ctrl+C cleanup completes quickly

## Files to Modify
- src/extensions.rs (apply budgets)
- src/config.rs (budget settings)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-04T21:13:04.320977250Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-04T21:13:04.331936080Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1yr1","depends_on_id":"bd-20lm","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-04T21:13:04.331895885Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2b1f","title":"Implement pi.registerCommand() for extension slash commands","description":"Allow extensions to register custom slash commands that integrate with the TUI.

## API Spec (from legacy)
```typescript
pi.registerCommand(name, {
  description: string,
  handler: (args, ctx) => Promise<void>,
  getArgumentCompletions?: (prefix) => CompletionItem[]
});
```

## Implementation Requirements
1. CommandRegistry in extension runtime to store registered commands
2. Hostcall handler for 'registerCommand' that validates and stores
3. Integration with autocomplete system (src/autocomplete.rs)
4. Integration with slash command processor (src/interactive.rs)
5. Command execution via extension runtime (JS handler)

## Data Flow
1. Extension calls pi.registerCommand('mycommand', {...})
2. Hostcall stores in CommandRegistry
3. /mycommand shows in autocomplete suggestions
4. User types /mycommand args
5. Interactive processor detects extension command
6. Invokes JS handler with args and ExtensionCommandContext

## Test Plan
- Unit test: registerCommand stores command metadata
- Integration test: registered command appears in autocomplete
- E2E test: /mycommand executes JS handler

- src/extensions_js.rs (hostcall handler)
- src/autocomplete.rs (include extension commands)
- src/interactive.rs (dispatch to extension handler)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-04T21:09:36.671947075Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-04T21:09:36.679838038Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2b1f","depends_on_id":"bd-2bnc","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-04T21:09:36.679801470Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2bis","title":"Task: Implement fixture-based conformance tests","description":"# Task: Implement Fixture-Based Conformance Tests

## Objective

Create Rust tests that replay captured TypeScript fixtures and verify identical behavior.

## Background

Fixtures captured from pi-mono provide ground truth. These tests replay the same inputs and verify outputs match exactly.

## Implementation

### File: tests/conformance/mod.rs

```rust
use std::fs;
use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};

#[derive(Debug, Deserialize)]
struct ConformanceSuite {
    suite: String,
    runtime: String,
    version: String,
    captured_at: String,
    cases: Vec<ConformanceCase>,
}

#[derive(Debug, Deserialize)]
struct ConformanceCase {
    name: String,
    input: serde_json::Value,
    expected: ExpectedResult,
}

#[derive(Debug, Deserialize)]
struct ExpectedResult {
    success: bool,
    data: Option<serde_json::Value>,
    error: Option<String>,
}

/// Load a conformance suite from fixture file.
fn load_suite(name: &str) -> ConformanceSuite {
    let path = format!("tests/conformance/fixtures/ts_outputs/{}.json", name);
    let content = fs::read_to_string(&path)
        .expect(&format!("Failed to load fixture: {}", path));
    serde_json::from_str(&content)
        .expect(&format!("Failed to parse fixture: {}", path))
}

/// Run all cases in a conformance suite.
async fn run_suite(suite: ConformanceSuite, runtime: &PiJsRuntime) -> Vec<TestResult> {
    let mut results = Vec::new();
    
    for case in suite.cases {
        let result = run_case(&case, runtime).await;
        results.push(result);
    }
    
    results
}

async fn run_case(case: &ConformanceCase, runtime: &PiJsRuntime) -> TestResult {
    // Parse input as hostcall request
    let request: HostcallRequest = serde_json::from_value(case.input.clone())
        .expect("Invalid hostcall request in fixture");
    
    // Execute through our dispatcher
    let dispatcher = runtime.hostcall_dispatcher();
    let actual = dispatcher.dispatch(request).await;
    
    // Compare result
    let passed = match (&case.expected, &actual) {
        (ExpectedResult { success: true, data: Some(expected), .. }, Ok(actual_data)) => {
            compare_json(expected, &actual_data)
        }
        (ExpectedResult { success: true, data: None, .. }, Ok(_)) => true,
        (ExpectedResult { success: false, error: Some(expected_err), .. }, Err(actual_err)) => {
            // Error message should match (or contain key parts)
            actual_err.to_string().contains(expected_err)
        }
        (ExpectedResult { success: false, .. }, Err(_)) => true,
        _ => false,
    };
    
    TestResult {
        name: case.name.clone(),
        passed,
        expected: format!("{:?}", case.expected),
        actual: format!("{:?}", actual),
    }
}

fn compare_json(expected: &serde_json::Value, actual: &serde_json::Value) -> bool {
    // Deep comparison, allowing for acceptable differences
    // (e.g., timestamp precision, ordering)
    expected == actual
}

#[derive(Debug)]
struct TestResult {
    name: String,
    passed: bool,
    expected: String,
    actual: String,
}
```

### Test Functions

```rust
#[cfg(test)]
mod conformance_tests {
    use super::*;
    
    #[tokio::test]
    async fn test_hostcall_tool_conformance() {
        let suite = load_suite("hostcall-tool");
        let runtime = create_test_runtime().await;
        
        let results = run_suite(suite, &runtime).await;
        
        for result in &results {
            if !result.passed {
                eprintln!("FAIL: {}", result.name);
                eprintln!("  Expected: {}", result.expected);
                eprintln!("  Actual:   {}", result.actual);
            }
        }
        
        let passed = results.iter().filter(|r| r.passed).count();
        let total = results.len();
        
        assert_eq!(passed, total, "{}/{} conformance tests passed", passed, total);
    }
    
    #[tokio::test]
    async fn test_hostcall_session_conformance() {
        let suite = load_suite("hostcall-session");
        let runtime = create_test_runtime().await;
        
        let results = run_suite(suite, &runtime).await;
        assert_all_passed(&results);
    }
    
    #[tokio::test]
    async fn test_hostcall_ui_conformance() {
        let suite = load_suite("hostcall-ui");
        let runtime = create_test_runtime().await;
        
        let results = run_suite(suite, &runtime).await;
        assert_all_passed(&results);
    }
    
    #[tokio::test]
    async fn test_event_dispatch_conformance() {
        let suite = load_suite("event-dispatch");
        let runtime = create_test_runtime().await;
        
        let results = run_suite(suite, &runtime).await;
        assert_all_passed(&results);
    }
    
    fn assert_all_passed(results: &[TestResult]) {
        let failures: Vec<_> = results.iter().filter(|r| !r.passed).collect();
        if !failures.is_empty() {
            for f in &failures {
                eprintln!("FAIL: {} - expected {} but got {}", f.name, f.expected, f.actual);
            }
            panic!("{} conformance test(s) failed", failures.len());
        }
    }
}
```

 {"id":"bd-5yyc","title":"Task: Implement turn lifecycle events (turn_start, turn_end)","description":"# Task: Implement Turn Lifecycle Events

## Objective

Dispatch turn_start and turn_end events around each agent turn (provider call + tool execution cycle).

## TypeScript Reference

```typescript
// turn_start
if (runner.hasHandlers('turn_start')) {
    await runner.emit({
        type: 'turn_start',
        sessionId: session.id,
        turnIndex: context.turnIndex,
    });
}

// turn_end
if (runner.hasHandlers('turn_end')) {
    await runner.emit({
        type: 'turn_end',
        sessionId: session.id,
        turnIndex: context.turnIndex,
        message: assistantMessage,
        toolResults: toolResults,
    });
}
```

## Events Covered

### turn_start Event
- **When**: Before calling provider.stream()
- **Purpose**: Observe/modify context, log turn start
- **Data**: session_id, turn_index

### turn_end Event
- **When**: After response and all tool results processed
- **Purpose**: Observe complete turn, log, analyze
- **Data**: session_id, turn_index, message (assistant), tool_results

## Implementation

```rust
impl Agent {
    async fn run_turn(&self, turn_index: usize, context: &mut Context) -> Result<TurnResult> {
        // Dispatch turn_start
        if let Some(dispatcher) = &self.event_dispatcher {
            if dispatcher.has_handlers("turn_start") {
                let event = ExtensionEvent::TurnStart {
                    session_id: self.session.id().to_string(),
                    turn_index,
                };
                dispatcher.dispatch::<()>(event).await?;
            }
        }
        
        // Call provider
        let response = self.provider.stream(/* ... */).await?;
        let assistant_message = self.process_response(response).await?;
        let tool_results = self.execute_tools(&assistant_message).await?;
        
        // Dispatch turn_end
        if let Some(dispatcher) = &self.event_dispatcher {
            if dispatcher.has_handlers("turn_end") {
                let event = ExtensionEvent::TurnEnd {
                    session_id: self.session.id().to_string(),
                    turn_index,
                    message: assistant_message.clone(),
                    tool_results: tool_results.clone(),
                };
                dispatcher.dispatch::<()>(event).await?;
            }
        }
        
        Ok(TurnResult { message: assistant_message, tool_results })
    }
}
```

5. test_tool_results_included_in_turn_end

-{"id":"bd-6vcm","title":"Feature: Extension Conformance Testing","description":"# Feature: Extension Conformance Testing

## Overview

Create a comprehensive conformance testing suite that verifies the Rust extension runtime behaves identically to the TypeScript reference implementation. This ensures 100% API compatibility.

## Importance

Extensions are a critical API surface. Third-party extensions must work identically in Rust and TypeScript. Conformance testing is the only way to guarantee this.

## Testing Strategy

### 1. Reference Capture (TypeScript)

Run test extensions against pi-mono (TypeScript) and capture:
- Hostcall request/response pairs
- Event handler invocations
- Tool registration results
- Error cases and messages

### 2. Fixture-Based Testing (Rust)

Replay the same scenarios in Rust and compare:
- Same inputs produce same outputs
- Same events fire at same points
- Same errors for invalid operations

### 3. Property-Based Testing

Use proptest to verify invariants:
- Hostcalls never panic
- Invalid inputs produce errors
- State consistency maintained

## Test Categories

### A. Hostcall Conformance

Test each hostcall type against reference:

| Hostcall | Test Cases |
|----------|------------|
| Tool | Register, execute, parameters, errors |
| Exec | Run command, capture output, exit codes |
| Http | GET/POST, headers, timeouts, errors |
| Session | Get messages, append, fork |
| Ui | Spinners, progress, notifications |
| Events | Subscribe, unsubscribe, dispatch |

### B. Event Hook Conformance

Test event dispatch matches reference:

| Event | Test Cases |
|-------|------------|
| tool_call | Blocking, non-blocking, errors |
| tool_result | Modification, pass-through |
| turn_start | Context access |
| turn_end | Message access |
| agent_start/end | Lifecycle timing |

### C. Error Conformance

Test error messages and codes match:
- Invalid hostcall type → specific error
- Missing parameters → specific error
- Permission denied → specific error
- Timeout → specific error

### D. Edge Cases

- Empty extension (no hooks)
- Extension with many hooks
- Concurrent hostcalls
- Large payloads
- Unicode in strings
- Null/undefined handling

```json
{
    "suite": "hostcall-tool",
    "cases": [
        {
            "name": "register_tool_basic",
            "input": {
                "type": "tool",
                "method": "register",

                    "name": "my-tool",
                    "description": "A test tool",
                    "parameters": { "type": "object" }
                }
            },
            "expected": {
                "success": true,
                "data": null
            }
        },
        {
            "name": "register_tool_missing_name",
            "input": {
                "type": "tool",
                "method": "register",
                "args": {
                    "description": "Missing name"
                }
            },
            "expected": {
                "success": false,
                "error": "Missing required field: name"
            }
        }
    ]
}
```

 {"id":"bd-yfs","title":"Implement configuration loading tests","description":"# Implement configuration loading tests

## Goal
Add unit tests for src/config.rs (332 lines, currently 0 tests).

## Background
Configuration affects all aspects of behavior:
- Default provider/model selection
- Compaction settings
- Retry behavior
- Shell path and prefix
- Terminal display options
- Thinking budgets

## Test Categories

### 1. Default Value Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn test_default_config() {
    let config = Config::default();
    
    assert_eq!(config.default_provider(), "anthropic");
    assert_eq!(config.default_model(), "claude-sonnet-4-20250514");
    assert_eq!(config.default_thinking_level(), ThinkingLevel::Off);
}

#[test]
fn test_compaction_defaults() {
    let config = Config::default();
    let comp = config.compaction();
    
    assert!(comp.enabled);
    assert_eq!(comp.reserve_tokens, 16384);
    assert_eq!(comp.keep_recent_tokens, 20000);
}

#[test]
fn test_retry_defaults() {
    let config = Config::default();
    let retry = config.retry();
    
    assert!(retry.enabled);
    assert_eq!(retry.max_retries, 3);
    assert_eq!(retry.base_delay_ms, 2000);
    assert_eq!(retry.max_delay_ms, 60000);
}
```

### 2. JSON Loading Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn test_load_config_from_json() {
    let json = r#"{
        "defaultProvider": "openai",
        "defaultModel": "gpt-4",
        "defaultThinkingLevel": "high"
    }"#;
    
    let config = Config::from_json(json).unwrap();
    
    assert_eq!(config.default_provider(), "openai");
    assert_eq!(config.default_model(), "gpt-4");
    assert_eq!(config.default_thinking_level(), ThinkingLevel::High);
}

#[test]
fn test_load_config_partial_json() {
    // Only some fields set - rest should use defaults
    let json = r#"{"defaultProvider": "openai"}"#;
    
    let config = Config::from_json(json).unwrap();
    
    assert_eq!(config.default_provider(), "openai");
    // Defaults for unset fields
    assert_eq!(config.default_model(), "claude-sonnet-4-20250514");
}

#[test]
fn test_load_config_invalid_json() {
    let json = "not valid json {";
    let result = Config::from_json(json);
    assert!(result.is_err());
}

#[test]
fn test_load_config_unknown_fields_ignored() {
    let json = r#"{"unknownField": "value", "defaultProvider": "anthropic"}"#;
    let config = Config::from_json(json).unwrap();
    assert_eq!(config.default_provider(), "anthropic");
}
```

### 3. File Loading Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn test_load_config_from_file() {
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
    let config_path = temp.path().join("settings.json");
    fs::write(&config_path, r#"{"defaultProvider": "gemini"}"#).unwrap();
    
    let config = Config::load_from_file(&config_path).unwrap();
    assert_eq!(config.default_provider(), "gemini");
}

#[test]
fn test_load_config_missing_file() {
    let result = Config::load_from_file(Path::new("/nonexistent/config.json"));
    // Should return default config, not error
    assert!(result.is_ok());
    assert_eq!(result.unwrap().default_provider(), "anthropic");
}
```

### 4. Merge Logic Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn test_merge_configs_project_overrides_global() {
    let global = Config::from_json(r#"{"defaultProvider": "openai"}"#).unwrap();
    let project = Config::from_json(r#"{"defaultProvider": "anthropic"}"#).unwrap();
    
    let merged = global.merge(&project);
    
    // Project should override global
    assert_eq!(merged.default_provider(), "anthropic");
}

#[test]
fn test_merge_configs_partial_override() {
    let global = Config::from_json(r#"{
        "defaultProvider": "openai",
        "defaultModel": "gpt-4"
    }"#).unwrap();
    let project = Config::from_json(r#"{
        "defaultProvider": "anthropic"
    }"#).unwrap();
    
    let merged = global.merge(&project);
    
    assert_eq!(merged.default_provider(), "anthropic");  // Overridden
    assert_eq!(merged.default_model(), "gpt-4");  // Inherited from global
}
```

### 5. Environment Variable Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn test_env_override_config_path() {
    std::env::set_var("PI_CONFIG_PATH", "/custom/path");
    let path = Config::config_path();
    assert_eq!(path, PathBuf::from("/custom/path"));
    std::env::remove_var("PI_CONFIG_PATH");
}

#[test]
fn test_env_api_keys() {
    std::env::set_var("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY", "sk-test");
    let key = Config::api_key_for_provider("anthropic");
    assert_eq!(key, Some("sk-test".to_string()));
    std::env::remove_var("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY");
}
```

### 6. Validation Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn test_invalid_thinking_level() {
    let json = r#"{"defaultThinkingLevel": "invalid"}"#;
    let result = Config::from_json(json);
    // Should either error or use default
}

#[test]
fn test_negative_retry_values() {
    let json = r#"{"retry": {"maxRetries": -1}}"#;
    let config = Config::from_json(json).unwrap();
    // Should clamp to 0 or use default
    assert!(config.retry().max_retries >= 0);
}
```

+{"id":"bd-ypyr","title":"Optional SQLite session backend for improved indexing","description":"Add optional SQLite backend for sessions using asupersync::database::sqlite.

## Current State
- Sessions stored as JSONL files
- Session index in separate SQLite for listing
- Search/filtering is limited

## Benefits of SQLite Backend
- Faster indexing and search
- Transaction-safe session compaction
- Full-text search on conversation content
- Concurrent multi-session access

## Implementation (Optional Feature)
1. Feature flag: 'sqlite-sessions'
2. SessionStore trait with JSONL and SQLite impls
3. Migration from JSONL to SQLite (optional)
4. Export back to JSONL for portability

## Schema Design
```sql
CREATE TABLE sessions (id, path, created_at, name, provider, model);
CREATE TABLE entries (session_id, id, parent_id, type, content, timestamp);
CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE entries_fts USING fts5(content);
```

## Backward Compatibility
- JSONL remains default
- SQLite opt-in via settings
- JSONL export always available

## Test Plan
- Unit test: SQLite storage CRUD
- Integration test: migration from JSONL
- Performance test: large session operations

- src/session.rs (SessionStore trait)
